next_head_excerpt:
# Chapter 13

The Thai place was on a side street south of campus, a narrow storefront between a dry cleaner and a vacant lot that had been vacant for as long as Doug had been coming here, which was fourteen years. The awning had faded from red to something closer to the color of salmon left out overnight, a defeated optimism Doug noticed every time and never mentioned because there was no one to mention it to. The same ceramic cat sat in the window with one paw raised, though it had migrated from the left side of the sill to the right at some point in 2019, and Doug had registered the move without understanding it. A bell rang when he pushed the door open. It was Saturday, just past noon.

Kelsey wasn't there yet. Doug was twenty minutes early, as he'd been at the diner in Worthington three months ago and at every parent-teacher conference and dentist appointment and claims assessment in the decades before that. He showed up early automatically, reflexively, because the alternative was arriving to conditions you hadn't assessed.

The woman at the front — Doug had been seeing her for fourteen years and did not know her name, a failure he'd compounded annually until it became irreversible — pointed him toward the booth in the back without being asked. She was wearing the jade bracelet she always wore, and reading glasses that were new since the last time he'd been here, thin gold frames that she looked over rather than through as she walked him to the table. The booth's vinyl had cracked along the right side where it met the wall, a split that had started small and widened s...
open_hooks_to_carry: Alex Chen's thesis (Doug as authentic masculinity symbol) meeting Doug's literal answers: 'What drives you?' 'I drive a Civic'
Alex following Doug through his actual life — the office, lunch with Roy, a session with Simone, the grocery store
prev_tail_excerpt:
Simone reached forward and clicked the recorder off. The click barely disturbed the room.

"I know," she said.

They sat until the light thinned. Simone finished her cold coffee. Then she packed the notebook and recorder into her canvas bag.

"Same time Monday," she said.

"Yeah."

At the door she half-turned back to him.

"The shampoo isn't for the set."

Doug looked at her.

"I'm writing it down because you didn't dress it up," she said. "That's different."

He nodded. "Okay."

She left. He heard her on the stairs, then the outside door.

The apartment went quiet. Her coffee cup was in the sink. Her pen cap had rolled onto the table while she was packing up. He picked it up and set it on the counter.

He went to the bathroom. He didn't turn on the shower. He looked at the ledge. His own shampoo sat there, blue cap, half empty.

Back in the kitchen, he poured a bowl of store-brand Raisin Bran and ate it standing at the counter. The milk was two days from expiration and still fine. He rinsed the bowl and spoon. He washed Simone's coffee cup and set it in the rack.

The pen cap stayed on the counter.
state_deltas_required: The journalism of personality — Alex constructing a narrative that Doug keeps accidentally dismantling by being honest
Another layer of fictionalized Doug is added — the profile's Doug is charmingly self-deprecating and quietly wise, and Doug recognizes nothing, and the machine feeds on the fiction
